  • The assignment of the valuation view is derived from the accounting principle defined in General Ledger customizing.
  • Depreciation area 99 is associated with ledger 4G, which is activated by scope item 5W2 – Group Valuation. Although depreciation area 99 is not included in the best practice content, it is automatically deployed based on the assignment of the accounting principle to ledger 4G in the General Ledger.
  • Tax depreciation area (15) is deployed only for local ledger.
  • Only one ledger can be defined as local ledger.

Frequently Asked Question related to Best Practice Content

Q1: Why does the best practice content not follow SAP recommendation?

A1: Leading ledger is delivered as local ledger with country specific accounting principle assigned at company code level as a solution for customer who are operating only in one country and will not need to report based on IFRS or USGP rules.

Q2: Why only one ledger can be defined as local ledger.

A2: Only one ledger can be defined as local ledger as only one tax depreciation area can be deployed.

entry.title: Clustering text documents using constrained clustering
entry.content:
Clustering is a fundamental technique in data analysis and machine learning, widely used for identifying and grouping similar objects within a dataset based on inherent characteristics or patterns. It involves partitioning data points into distinct subsets known as clusters, where data points within the same cluster are more similar to each other than to those in other clusters. This unsupervised learning approach facilitates data exploration, pattern recognition, and information retrieval across various fields, from market segmentation to image analysis. However, traditional clustering methods operate without prior knowledge about the data, often leading to challenges in achieving meaningful or contextually relevant groupings. This limitation paves the way for constrained clustering, an advanced form of clustering that incorporates domain-specific constraints or prior information to guide the clustering process, ensuring more accurate and meaningful results tailored to specific analytical needs.

We are excited to announce the integration of a newly developed constrained clustering algorithm into SAP HANA Predictive Analysis Library (PAL). In this blog post, we will explore the fundamentals of constrained clustering and demonstrate its application in document clustering.

 

Constrained clustering

As data complexity increases, traditional clustering algorithms such as k-means and hierarchical clustering often fall short in capturing the intrinsic structure of the data. Deep clustering addresses these limitations by combining deep learning and clustering techniques to provide powerful representations and facilitate more accurate clustering. This approach serves as an excellent foundation for developing constrained clustering algorithms, which incorporate domain-specific constraints into the clustering process.

Deep Embedded Clustering (DEC) is a framework that leverages deep neural networks to learn low-dimensional representations of data, which are subsequently used for clustering. The key idea behind DEC is to use a deep autoencoder to obtain meaningful embeddings and then refine these embeddings through clustering-oriented learning. The components of DEC contains:

  1. Autoencoder Architecture: An autoencoder is a type of neural network designed to learn efficient codings of input data. It consists of two parts: the encoder, which compresses the data, and the decoder, which reconstructs the data from the compressed representation. In DEC, the encoder is crucial for learning compact, informative representations that can be clustered effectively.
  2. Clustering Layer: Once a suitable representation is obtained from the encoder, DEC introduces a clustering layer that refines these representations to better fit a clustering model.
  3. Optimization Objective: The DEC framework focuses on minimizing the reconstruction loss of the autoencoder along with a clustering loss that aims to improve clustering accuracy.

Constrained clustering is an extension of traditional clustering paradigms that incorporates domain-specific knowledge into the clustering process through constraints. This approach is particularly beneficial in scenarios where certain data points are known to belong together or certain separations should be maintained. By building on the DEC framework, constrained clustering can effectively leverage deep representations while respecting additional constraints. To integrate constraints into DEC, important modifications include:

  1. Defining Constraints: Constraints can be pairwise, such as must-link constraints (indicating that two data points should be clustered together) and cannot-link constraints (indicating that two data points should belong to different clusters). Constraints can also be triplet constraint by specifying relative similarity relationships among data points, where a given anchor point should be more similar to a designated positive point than to a negative point.
  2. Modified Clustering Layer: In the DEC algorithm that accounts for constraints, the clustering layer can be extended to handle these constraints. This involves modifying the clustering loss to not only minimize the disparity within clusters but also satisfy the specified constraints.
  3. Optimization with Constraints: The optimization objective of constrained clustering balances between the fidelity of data representation, clustering quality, and adherence to constraints. This involves sophisticated optimization techniques such as alternating optimization to balance these competing aspects effectively.

In the following section, we will explore the application of constrained clustering in the context of document clustering.

 

Example

Document clustering is a crucial process in the realm of natural language processing (NLP) and information retrieval. It involves grouping text documents into clusters, where documents in the same cluster share similar topics or contents. This technique has vast applications in organizing, filtering, and searching information, thus enhancing user experience in navigating vast text archives. The dataset for applying document clustering is the 20 Newsgroups dataset.

Dataset

The 20 Newsgroups dataset is a benchmark dataset used extensively for text classification and clustering tasks. It comprises approximately 20,000 newsgroup documents, partitioned across 20 different newsgroups, each corresponding to a different topic. This dataset serves as a useful test bed for document clustering due to its diversity in topics and ease of accessibility. These topics range broadly from computer science themes such as "comp.graphics" to recreational or opinion-based themes like "rec.sport.baseball."

Before applying clustering algorithms, documents must be preprocessed to transform the text into a suitable format for analysis. This includes:

- Tokenization: Breaking down text into individual words or tokens.

- Stopwords Removal: Excluding common words that do not contribute to topic differentiation (e.g., "the," "is," "and").

- Stemming/Lemmatization: Reducing words to their base or root form (e.g., "running" to "run").

- Vectorization: Converting text into numerical vectors using techniques like TF-IDF or embeddings. Interested readers can find more information on text embeddings in the topics linked at the bottom of the page.

Once the document data has been vectorized, we can proceed to prepare the constraints information. For 20 Newsgroups datasets that includes target information, we employ a straightforward strategy to automatically generate constraints.

Pairwise constraints

We randomly select pairs of instances to generate pairwise constraints. To ensure the constraints are transitive, we calculate the transitive closure for all instances that are must-linked. Subsequently, we derive additional constraints from the cannot-link constraints to maintain this property. The resulting constraints are entered into the constraints table. In this table, the first column specifies the type of constraint, with '1' representing a must-link and '-1' representing a cannot-link. The second and third columns contain the IDs of the instance pairs. Here is an example of a pairwise constraint table for illustration:

CONSTRAINT_TYPEID1ID2
101
-112
-123

Triplet constraints

Triplet constraints indicate that instance i is more similar to instance j than to instance k. To emulate human input on these triplet constraints, we randomly choose instances to serve as anchors (i). For each anchor, we then randomly select two instances (j and k) based on their similarity to the anchor. This similarity is determined by calculating the Euclidean distance between the preprocessed embeddings of the instances. The following is an example of generated triplet constraints specifying anchor, positive and nagetive instances:

ANCHORPOSITIVENAGETIVE
123
245
367

Once the input data and constraints are prepared, using constrained clustering is as straightforward as employing any other algorithm in the PAL. Below is an annotated code example for your reference.

--########## COLUMN TABLE CREATION ##########
CREATE LOCAL TEMPORARY COLUMN TABLE #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL ("PARAM_NAME" NVARCHAR (256), "INT_VALUE" INTEGER, "DOUBLE_VALUE" DOUBLE, "STRING_VALUE" NVARCHAR (1000));
CREATE COLUMN TABLE PAL_DCC_PREMODEL_TBL ("ROW_INDEX" INTEGER, "MODEL_CONTENT" NVARCHAR (5000));
CREATE COLUMN TABLE PAL_DCC_RESULT_TBL ("ID" INTEGER, "CLUSTER_ID" INTEGER, "CONFIDENCE" DOUBLE);
CREATE COLUMN TABLE PAL_DCC_MODEL_TBL ("ROW_INDEX" INTEGER, "MODEL_CONTENT" NVARCHAR (5000));
CREATE COLUMN TABLE PAL_DCC_LOG_TBL ("EPOCH" INTEGER, "BATCH" NVARCHAR (256), "VALUE" DOUBLE);
CREATE COLUMN TABLE PAL_DCC_STATISTICS_TBL ("STAT_NAME" NVARCHAR (256), "STAT_VALUE" NVARCHAR (1000), "STAT_VECTOR" REAL_VECTOR);

--########## TABLE INSERTS ##########
-- Assume the data is inserted in the PAL_DCC_DATA_TBL and the constraints are in the PAL_DCC_CONSTRAINTS_TBL table.

--########## PAL_PARAMETER_TBL DATA INSERTION ##########
-- mandatory, number of groups
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('GROUP_NUMBER', 4, NULL, NULL);
-- mandatory, type of constraints
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('CONSTRAINT_TYPE', 0, NULL, NULL);
-- hidden layer sizes of encoder
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('ENCODER_HIDDEN_DIMS', NULL, NULL, '50, 200');
-- dimension of latent space
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('EMBEDDING_DIM', 3, NULL, NULL);
-- whether to use normalization
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('NORMALIZATION', 1, NULL, NULL);
-- seed for random number generator
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('SEED', 1, NULL, NULL);
-- learning rate of pretraining
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('PRETRAIN_LEARNING_RATE', NULL, 0.01, NULL);
-- number of pretraining epochs
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('PRETRAIN_EPOCHS', 200, NULL, NULL);
-- number of training samples in a batch
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('PRETRAIN_BATCH_SIZE', 16, NULL, NULL);
-- ratio of total number of threads that can be used
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('THREAD_RATIO', NULL, 1.0, NULL);
-- degree of distorting latent space
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('GAMMA', NULL, 0.1, NULL);
-- pairwise, penalty for must-link constraints
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('ML_PENALTY', NULL, 0.1, NULL);
-- pairwise, penalty for cannot-link constraints
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('CL_PENALTY', NULL, 1.0, NULL);
-- triplet, margin in triplet loss
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('THETA', NULL, 0.1, NULL);
-- learning rate
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('LEARNING_RATE', NULL, 0.01, NULL);
-- maximum number of training epochs
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('MAX_EPOCHS', 100, NULL, NULL);
-- number of training samples in a batch
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('BATCH_SIZE', 16, NULL, NULL);
-- frequency of updating target distribution
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('UPDATE_INTERVAL', 1, NULL, NULL);
-- pairwise, number of must-link constraints in a batch
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('ML_BATCH_SIZE', 16, NULL, NULL);
-- pairwise, number of cannot-link constraints in a batch
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('CL_BATCH_SIZE', 16, NULL, NULL);
-- triplet, number of triplet constraints in a batch
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('TRIPLET_BATCH_SIZE', 16, NULL, NULL);
-- pairwise, frequency of training with must-link constraints
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('ML_UPDATE_INTERVAL', 1, NULL, NULL);
-- pairwise, frequency of training with cannot-link constraints
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('CL_UPDATE_INTERVAL', 1, NULL, NULL);
-- triplet, frequency of training with triplet constraints
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('TRIPTLET_UPDATE_INTERVAL', 1, NULL, NULL);
-- stopping threshold
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('TOLERANCE', NULL, 0.01, NULL);
-- verbosity of log
INSERT INTO #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('VERBOSE', 0, NULL, NULL);

--########## PAL_PIPELINE_EXPLAIN FOR TIME SERIES CALL ##########
DO BEGIN
    lt_data = SELECT * FROM PAL_DCC_DATA_TBL;
    lt_constraints = SELECT * FROM PAL_DCC_CONSTRAINTS_TBL;
    lt_param = SELECT * FROM #PAL_PARAMETER_TBL;
    lt_premodel = SELECT * FROM PAL_DCC_PREMODEL_TBL;
    CALL _SYS_AFL.PAL_CONSTRAINED_CLUSTERING(:lt_data, :lt_constraints, :lt_param, :lt_premodel, lt_result, lt_model, lt_log, lt_statistics);
    INSERT INTO PAL_DCC_RESULT_TBL SELECT * FROM :lt_result;
    INSERT INTO PAL_DCC_MODEL_TBL SELECT * FROM :lt_model;
    INSERT INTO PAL_DCC_LOG_TBL SELECT * FROM :lt_log;
    INSERT INTO PAL_DCC_STATISTICS_TBL SELECT * FROM :lt_statistics;
END;

--########## SELECT * TABLES ##########
SELECT * FROM PAL_DCC_RESULT_TBL;
SELECT * FROM PAL_DCC_MODEL_TBL;
SELECT * FROM PAL_DCC_LOG_TBL;

You can view the clustering results, model, and training log in the output tables. Below are illustrative snapshots of the output tables.

The result table presents the assigned cluster ID for each instance and includes a third column that indicates the soft label or probabilities.

The model table documents crucial training information and the weights for the trained neural networks.

The entire training process can be monitored through the log table, which contains loss values for each training iteration. Additionally, the verbosity of the log can be adjusted using the VERBOSE parameter.

 

Summary

Deep clustering provides a robust framework that pushes the boundaries of unsupervised learning through the integration of deep learning. By extending DEC to incorporate constraints, we show a variant that combines the strengths of deep representations with domain-specific knowledge, enhancing clustering applicability in complex real-world problems.

 

Recent topics on HANA machine learning:

Hybrid Prediction with Tabular and Text Inputs using Hybrid Gradient Boosting Trees 

Dimensionality Reduction of Text Embeddings for Hybrid Prediction Data 

Text Embedding Service in SAP HANA Cloud Predictive Analysis Library (PAL) 

Document Clustering using KMeans and Text Embeddings 

New Text Analysis in SAP HANA Cloud Predictive Analysis Library (PAL) 

New Information Retrieval Techniques in SAP HANA Cloud using BM25 and ANNS for Advanced Text Mining 

Text Chunking – An Exciting New NLP Function in SAP HANA Cloud 

Exploring ML Explainability in SAP HANA PAL - AutoML

entry.title: Re: Concern in Derivation T. Code KEDR for Margin Analysis
entry.content:
As far as I know, KEDR will provide the rules for deriving characteristics rather than segment numbers. The system will then create or update a segment based on these characteristics, after which the number will be defined either within the COPA_OBJ number range (T code SNRO), or based on a combination of the characteristic values. (There are almost no user transactions that display the segment number.)

However, you could use a KEDR enhancement to identify or select a profitability segment model according to your criteria, read its derived characteristic values, and copy back all the necessary characteristics.

entry.title: Maintain PIR APP Sales quantity
entry.content:
In our system, we have 2 MRP areas for same plant.

Now when we are creating a sales order by mentioning both Plant and Storage location.

We expect that Maintain PIR App will show Sales Quantity in MRP Area 2, but it is showing Sales quantity in MRP area ABC ( Plant directly)

 

When we are doing Goods Issues, withdrawal quantity is correctly updated to MRP AREA 2.

 

So we are not able to do accuracy calculation.

 

Please assist on the same.

entry.title: FAQs: Get Started With the NSE Advisor in SAP HANA Platform 2.0 SPS 08
entry.content:
If you're an SAP professional or an enterprise leveraging SAP HANA, you might have come across the term 'NSE advisor' and wondered what it is, how it works, and how it can benefit your organization. In this blog post, we aim to answer some frequently asked questions that will help you make the most of this tool and gradually transfer your tables into NSE (Native Storage Extension) with the help of the NSE advisor. For more information, you can also refer to the the official documentation and the whitepaper. 

Q1: What is the NSE advisor?

The NSE advisor is a tool that provides recommendations on load units for tables, partitions, or columns, based on their access frequency during a given workload. It helps you determine which data should be loaded into the buffer cache (NSE) for quick access (page-loadable) and which data should be kept in memory using columnar storage to enhance performance (column-loadable).

The NSE advisor assesses the temperature of the data based on its access frequency and uses rule-based heuristics to identify hot and warm objects as candidates for these optimizations.

Q2: How to configure the NSE advisor for the first use?

There are several ways to run the NSE advisor. In this blogpost, we refer to using the built-in procedure. For more information, see Using the NSE Advisor with SQL Commands (Built-in Procedure) for Warm Data Recommendations.

To make the most of the NSE advisor you need to be familiar with your data-access patterns and understand your workload to find out when it is the most beneficial time to run it. However, you might not have this knowledge yet, so you should begin with somehow conservative settings. For example, set the HOT_OBJECT_THRESHOLD to 15%, the COLD_OBJECT_THRESHOLD to 15%, and set the DURATION value in seconds.

The DURATION is normally defined according to your workload; customers’ workloads normally have periodicity, so the DURATION should cover one cycle of your workload. To decide the value for DURATION, you should consider your primary business usage time. If you are not sure of your primary business usage time, you can analyze it with M_LOAD_HISTORY_HOST, M_LOAD_HISTORY_INFO, or M_LOAD_HISTORY_SERVICE.

In this blogpost we’ll assume your business usage time is 1 day (86400 seconds). So you can run the following statement: CALL SYS.CS_NSE_ADVISOR(ACTION=>'ENABLE', HOT_OBJECT_THRESHOLD=>'15', COLD_OBJECT_THRESHOLD=>'15', MIN_OBJECT_SIZE=>'1048576', DURATION=>'86400', REQUEST_ID=>?)

Q3: How to apply recommendations?

By now, you should have your first set of recommendations. You can retrieve the recommendations from the M_CS_NSE_ADVISOR view. Pay attention to the "confidence" value, which helps you to prioritize what recommendations to apply first. Recommendations should not be implemented all at once, rather they should be applied step by step, starting with those with the highest confidence value.  

To diminish disruption, implement recommendations during your system's downtime or when the system isn't heavily in use—for example, early morning or late night, depending on your business hours. After applying the recommendations, you can monitor the performance of your SAP HANA servers by one or two cycles of your workload, for example, 1 or 2 days.  See Q4 of this blog post.

After applying each recommendation you should verify and adjust the maximum NSE buffer cache size, as well as the unload_threshold parameter value (if not set to be automatically calculated). 

If you encounter performance issues on a table (maybe because it changed to PAGE LOADABLE and has a low performance), you can revert its load unit and apply a recommendation rule on it. This way, the NSE advisor will not suggest recommendations for that table anymore. See Q6 from this blog post.

Q4: How can I determine if my configuration settings are fine-tuned to suit my workload?

A fine-tuned configuration balances performance and memory usage efficiently. Here are some indicators:

  • Query Performance: You may notice slightly reduced performance on warm data (PAGE LOADABLE) compared to hot data (COLUMN LOADABLE), but this is expected as it consumes less memory.
  • Functionality: All tables can theoretically be put into Native Store Extension (NSE) without functional errors. However, your applications should still perform adequately.
  • Resource Management: A well-configured system allows you to place a sufficient number of tables into NSE without encountering performance degradation or out-of-memory issues.

If you struggle to achieve a satisfactory cost/performance balance using NSE features for your workload, it might be beneficial to consider upgrading your SAP HANA system with additional CPU or memory resources.

Q5: How I can I fine-tune configuration settings to fit my workload?

To get a better feel of how the advisor works, enable it for a period, apply the recommendations according to your needs, and monitor the impact they have on the performance of your instances. With this information in mind, you can gradually increase the value of the HOT_OBJECT_THRESHOLD or COLD_OBJECT_THRESHOLD. For example, you can set the starting value to 15%, in the second round you can increase it to 20%, and in the third round to 25%. Modify these values and apply recommendations until you achieve a balance of cost/performance in your system. 

Q6: Can the advisor provide unexpected recommendations?

Yes, it is possible to receive unusual recommendations from the advisor under certain circumstances. The Native Store Extension (NSE) advisor uses access counters and size information to analyse access patterns in your workload. It relies on two thresholds to distinguish between hot and cold areas of your tables. Here are a couple of situations where recommendations might fluctuate:

  • Borderline Cases: Some tables may lie near the boundaries of these hot and cold areas. As a result, the advisor's recommendations for these objects might change frequently.
  • Dynamic Workloads: If your workload patterns change frequently or cyclically, the advisor's recommendations may also vary to reflect these shifts.

Therefore, it is essential to monitor and evaluate the advisor's recommendations in the context of your specific workload and installed configuration.

If you want to exclude certain tables, partitions, or columns from getting recommendations, you can use recommendation rules. For more information, see Recommendation Rules.

We hope these answers help you get started with the NSE advisor. Do you have more questions? Feel free to leave them in the comments below and we'll address them too!

entry.title: Can someone help me with this question about SAP Signavio?
entry.content:
Scenario: A bank aims to reduce the processing time of loan applications. Which factors should be analyzed using process mining to achieve this goal?
There are 3 correct answers to this question.

Response:

 

A

Interest rates applied to processed loans

 

B

Average customer wait time

 

C

Time taken for each step of the application

 

D

Number of applications processed per staff member

What would be the correct answers from above? I think it should be B, C and D, but I'm not sure.

entry.title: Electronic Documents Through VERI*FACTU in Spain
entry.content:
(Scroll down for the English version)

Que es VERI*FACTU?

En julio de 2021, el gobierno español aprobó la 11/2021 que introduce un conjunto de medidas para prevenir y combatir el fraude fiscal. Una de estas medidas obliga tanto a las empresas desarrolladoras como a los usuarios de sistemas informáticos que soportan procesos de facturación a garantizar la integridad, conservación, accesibilidad, legibilidad, trazabilidad e inalterabilidad de los registros. Esto debe hacerse sin interpolaciones, omisiones o alteraciones, de las cuales no quede constancia adecuada en los sistemas mismos.

Los contribuyentes pueden cumplir este requisito utilizando una de las siguientes dos opciones:

  • Estar acogido al Suministro Inmediato de Información (SII), ya sea porque la empresa está obligada a ello o porque se ha acogido voluntariamente al SII.
  • Implementar un sistema informático de emisión de facturas verificables (VERI*FACTU).

Es importante aclarar que esta normativa no se refiere a la facturación electrónica per se, sino al envío de información fiscal específica a la Agencia Tributaria.

Las empresas deberán adaptarse a esta normativa antes de enero de 2026, mientras que los profesionales tendrán hasta julio de 2026.

Fuente: Agencia Estatal de Administracion Tributaria (AEAT)

Características Principales de un Sistema VERI*FACTU

  • Creación simultánea de un registro de factura al momento de emitir o cancelar una factura.
  • Cada registro contará con una huella única para evitar su manipulación.
  • Cada uno de los registros deberá estar encadenado cronológicamente.
  • Envío simultáneo a la Agencia Tributaria de los registros.
  • Las facturas en papel deben incluir un código QR para que el receptor pueda verificar con su móvil si la factura ha sido enviada a la AEAT.

Cómo Prepararse para Adaptarse a esta Normativa

Las compañías deben realizar un análisis sobre aquellas empresas de su organización que no estén actualmente acogidas al SII, y decidir si adoptarán el SII a partir de enero de 2026 o implementarán un sistema de emisión de facturas verificables.

Si optan por un sistema VERI*FACTU, pueden considerar la solución basada en SAP Document and Reporting Compliance Cloud Edition, que garantiza el cumplimiento de la normativa para soluciones SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP S/4HANA y SAP ERP mediante las siguientes capacidades:

  • Generar registros de factura estructurados conforme a la normativa
  • Automatizar el proceso de envío de registros a la AEAT
  • Incluir los códigos QR en la impresión de facturas
  • Monitor detallado de la cadena de registros creados, enviados y con su estado

Diagrama de flujo de datos

Para detalles técnicos de la solución VERI*FACTU revisar la nota: 3585725 - Electronic Document Processing for Spain VERI*FACTU: Implementation Overview

Para un resumen de los puntos más relevantes de esta normativa, consulta el KBA; 3609812 - FAQ - Electronic Document Processing for Spain VERI*FACTU legal change 2025/2026

Para los próximos cambios legales relativos a VERI*FACTU, la recomendación es utilizar nuestro Regulatory Change Manager, donde puede encontrar el estado más reciente de esta normativa.

***

What is VERI*FACTU?

In July 2021, the Spanish government approved the   11/2021 introducing a set of measures to prevent and combat tax fraud. One of these measures requires both software providers companies and users of computer systems supporting invoicing processes to ensure the integrity, storage, accessibility, readability, traceability, and immutability of records, without interpolations, omissions, or alterations that are not adequately documented in the systems themselves.

Taxpayers can meet this requirement by using one of the following two options:

  • Being subscribed to the Immediate Supply of Information (SII), either because the company is obliged to do so or has voluntarily adopted the SII.
  • Implementing a computer system for issuing verifiable invoices (VERI*FACTU).

It is important to clarify that this regulation does not refer to electronic invoicing per se but to the transmission of specific fiscal information to the Tax Agency.

Companies must adapt to this regulation by January 2026, while professionals can do it until July 2026.

Source: State Tax Administration Agency (AEAT)

Key Features of a VERI*FACTU System

  • Simultaneous creation of an invoice record when issuing or canceling an invoice.
  • Each record will have a unique footprint to prevent manipulation.
  • Each record must be chained chronologically.
  • Simultaneous transmission of records to the Tax Agency.
  • Paper invoices must include a QR code so the recipient can verify with their mobile device whether the invoice has been sent to the AEAT.

How to Prepare for Adapting to This Regulation

 

Companies need to analyze those entities within their organization that are not currently subscribed to the SII, and decide whether they will adopt the SII starting January 2026 or implement a system for issuing verifiable invoices.

If they opt for a VERI*FACTU system, they can consider the solution based on SAP Document and Reporting Compliance Cloud Edition, which ensures compliance with regulations for SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP S/4HANA, and SAP ERP solutions through the following capabilities:

  • Generate structured invoice records in accordance with regulations
  • Automate the process of sending records to the AEAT
  • Include QR codes in invoice printing

Detailed monitoring of the chain of records created, sent, and their status

Data flow diagram

For technical details of the VERI*FACTU solution, review the note: 3585725 - Electronic Document Processing for Spain VERI*FACTU: Implementation Overview.

For a summary of the most relevant points of this regulation, refer to KBA: 3609812 - FAQ - Electronic Document Processing for Spain VERI*FACTU legal change 2025/2026.

For upcoming legal changes of VERI*FACTU, the recommendation is to use our Regulatory Change Manager, where you can find the latest status of this regulation.

entry.title: Re: How can I find the source field of a target field in...
entry.content:
Hi,

The ‘VAT Registration No.’ field in the billing document header is sourced from the business partner master data. Specifically, it is derived from the VAT registration number maintained for the business partner Poland - Maintaining VAT Registration Number for Business Partners in the ‘Manage Business Partner Master Data’ app.

Source Field

  • Source Field: The VAT registration number is stored in the business partner master data under the ‘Tax Numbers’ section.

Business Partner Role

  • Business Partner Role: The VAT registration number can be associated with different roles such as Payer, BillTo, or ShipTo. The determination of which role’s VAT registration number is used can depend on the configuration settings for the sales organization and the specific sales document.

Best regards, Dequan Xu

entry.title: Re: How to Use Vendor Sub-Ranges in SAP MM for Multi-Currency Procurement
entry.content:
Hi Leelamohan, 

Thanks for the explanation, but I must say that I disagree with your logic. The vendor subrange is not really meant for this. It helps you to divide the vendor's product range. Buying in different currencies can be dealt with in a much more automated way. You can maintain vendor-material inforecords on plant level, for example and the price on that same level. In that case everything will be determined automatically. It is also crucial to maintain your purchasing organisations on the right level. If a company has operational procurement locally but strategical procurement globally, then you should maintain a Purchase organisation locally for buying and a global one for pricing, where the global prices and contracts can be used by the local entity. This delivers much more automation, but also detailed process information. It will still allow you to use the same vendor master for all procurement. 

Kind regards, 

Sjaak

entry.title: Re: Do materials involved in third-party transactions nee...
entry.content:
Hi,

In SAP S/4HANA, maintaining the gross weight for materials is often required Supply Chain - Packaging Material for various processes, including third-party transactions. This requirement is typically due to the need for accurate logistics and transportation management, where the gross weight plays a crucial role Transportation Management ™ - Verified Gross Mass for a Container in determining shipping costs and compliance with regulations.

Is It Mandatory?

Yes, maintaining the gross weight for materials used in third-party processes is generally mandatory. This is because the gross weight is essential for calculating freight charges and ensuring proper handling during transportation. The system uses this information to manage logistics efficiently and comply with international shipping standards.

Best regards, Dequan Xu

entry.title: How to change the pk naming convention to <table name>_PK
entry.content:
Hello

In SQL preview, the PK naming convention is not what expected.  it looks like:

constraint PK_<table name> primary key (<list of columns>)

How can I change the pk naming convention to <table name>_PK?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: How to change the pk naming convention to <table name...
entry.content:
Hello,

Please follow these steps:

  1. Select Database > Edit Current DBMS.
  2. Select ::Script\Objects\PKey\ConstName.
  3. Change the default value PK_%.U27:TABLE%
    to %.U27:TABLE%_PK
  4. Click OK to save and close the DBMS resource file.

Kind regards

entry.title: Utilization of SAP Integration suite to incorporate satellite IOT data into SAP Systems.
entry.content:
Hi All,

We can be able to integrate SAP Integration suite with the satellite IoT Data Providers which is an innovative as well as an emerging concept that has vast applications in logistics, defense and agriculture. It involves capturing of the data from satellites which includes geolocation, weather and asset movement while integrating it into the SAP systems like S/4 Hana, EWM and TM via SAP Integration Suite.


USECASE:

Imagining a logistics company with the fleets operating in some remote areas where GSM/4G signals are not available. Now, satellite IoT devices are often installed on the devices that could send telemetry data like location, temperature via satellite to a cloud provider or ground station. SAP Integration suite can then
ingest, process and roue this data into the SAP backend systems.

Architecture Overview:

 

Technical Flow:

i-Flow Design Steps in SAP Integration Suite:​

  1. Sender: HTTP Receiver from cloud IoT platform with the JSON payload.​
  2. Content Modifier: Extracting GPS, temperature as well as timestamp​
  3. Router: For applying the conditional logic.​
  4. Mapping: Conversion of the data type formats which can include JSON-> XML or it can be JSON-> SAP OData Format.​
  5. Receiver: Pushes data  into SAP backend using HTTP or OData destination.​

TESTING:​

We can simulate IoT platform using tools like Postman.

Conclusion:​

This integration would open doors to the smarter asset tracking as well as real-time decision making areas where GSM (Global System for Mobile Communication) is not available.  Hence, SAP Integration Suite makes it possible to diminish the gap between remote IoT data as well as the enterprise systems.

entry.title: Reusable SAP CPI Interface for Proxies Using Groovy: Simple Proxy XML to JSON Integration
entry.content:
Introduction

This blog will show you how to create a single interface in SAP CPI that can be reused for processing proxy XML data from SAP S4/SAP systems or any other systems also.

Use Case

We will come across situations where we need to send different sets of data with different XML structures from SAP or SAP S4 to a target application using SAP CPI.

Normally, we design a specific proxy structure for XML that the ABAP team can use to send data to the middleware. But when there are multiple XML structures and the data needs to be sent separately from the source system, we often need to create multiple proxy structures. Also, if a field name needs to be changed or a new field needs to be added, the interface must be updated each time.

I came across this situation where SAP needed to send different XML messages, and the ABAP team asked for multiple proxy structures and changes. Each different message was going to the same target application but with a different resource path. This is going to take lot of time and different interfaces are needed as each message use different path in the HTTP.

To avoid this, I used a standardized XML format with simple <FieldName> and <FieldValue> pairs, and I added a resource path identifier in the header. This way, I was able to create just one reusable interface in SAP CPI that could handle all the different incoming messages, which I then sent to the target application.

Architecture

Development Steps

 

  1. Standardize Input XML in SAP

I have created Standardize SAP proxy structures into a common, uniform format with header

<FieldName> and <FieldValue> XML pair structure

 

This contains Header section I will be using to identify my resource path

In SAP CPI , I have used content modifier to capture the proxy header table data and saved it in property to use in HTTP receiver adapter. This property dynamically call the endpoint.

 

  1. Groovy Script in CPI for XML to Json

This Groovy script parses the incoming XML, extracts field-value pairs, and builds a JSON structure:

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;
import java.util.HashMap;
import groovy.xml.*
import groovy.json.*
def Message processData(Message message)
{
    def xml = message.getBody(java.lang.String)
    def records = new XmlSlurper().parseText(xml)
    def jsonList = records.Record.collect { record ->
    def map = [:]
    record.Item.each { item ->
        map[item.FieldName.text()] = item.FieldValue.text()
    }
    return map
    }
    def json = JsonOutput.prettyPrint(JsonOutput.toJson(jsonList))
    message.setBody(json)
    return message;
}

 

  1.  Post Transformed JSON Data to Target Endpoint

    HTTP Adapter configuration: In previous Content Modifier I have used Tablename to dynamically identify target URL.

     

     

     

Testing

Input XML1

<message>
	<Record>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>MANDT</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>300</FieldValue>
		</Item>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>CLINT</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>1234</FieldValue>
		</Item>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>KLART</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>003</FieldValue>
		</Item>
	</Record>
</message>

Output Json1:

[
	{
		"MANDT": "300",
		"CLINT": "1234",
		"KLART": "003"
	}
]

example2:

Input XML

<message>
	<Record>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>MANDT</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>300</FieldValue>
		</Item>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>TPLNR</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>001A</FieldValue>
		</Item>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>SPRAS</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>E</FieldValue>
		</Item>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>PLTXT</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>CheckPLTXTValueRecord1</FieldValue>
		</Item>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>PLTXU</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>CheckPLTXUTValueRec1</FieldValue>
		</Item>
	</Record>
	<Record>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>MANDT</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>300</FieldValue>
		</Item>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>TPLNR</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>001B</FieldValue>
		</Item>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>SPRAS</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>E</FieldValue>
		</Item>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>PLTXT</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>CheckPLTXTValueRecord2</FieldValue>
		</Item>
		<Item>
			<FieldName>PLTXU</FieldName>
			<FieldValue>CheckPLTXUTValueRec2</FieldValue>
		</Item>
	</Record>
</message>

OutputJson:

[
    {
        "MANDT": "300",
        "TPLNR": "001A",
        "SPRAS": "E",
        "PLTXT": "CheckPLTXTValueRecord1",
        "PLTXU": "CheckPLTXUTValueRec1"
    },
    {
        "MANDT": "300",
        "TPLNR": "001B",
        "SPRAS": "E",
        "PLTXT": "CheckPLTXTValueRecord2",
        "PLTXU": "CheckPLTXUTValueRec2"
    }
]

 

Take Away

  1. This design reusable, scalable integration strategy in SAP CPI using Groovy scripting and a generalized XML structure for simple transmissions.
  2. Implementing a single interface capable of handling multiple tables data with dynamic fields, this solution simplifies integration architecture, accelerates delivery, and reduces ongoing maintenance.
  3. Finally it eliminates the need of create multiple interfaces for similar data transmissions if we could able to handle simple design approcahes in SAP CPI.

entry.title: Re: Sales return generating multiple Nota Fiscal
entry.content:
Hi,

When you perform a sales return transaction with ‘Refund Type: Credit Memo’ and ‘Refund Control: Create Credit Memo’, the system is expected to generate a credit memo for the returned items. However, if a correction invoice is generated unexpectedly, it might be due to specific configurations Processing Return with a Customer Nota Fiscal Avulsa (NFA-e) or processes related to the handling of credit memos and returns.

Possible Causes:

  1. Configuration Settings: The system might be configured to automatically generate correction invoices under certain conditions, such as discrepancies in pricing or quantity.
  2. Process Flow: The creation of a correction invoice could be part of a standard process flow for handling returns and refunds, Claims, Returns, and Refund Management especially if there are adjustments needed for the original transaction.

Steps to Resolve:

  • Review Configuration: Check the configuration settings related to credit memo processing and returns to ensure they align with your expectations.

Best regards, Dequan Xu

entry.title: SAP SAC Intgerated Financial Planning Calculating product costs
entry.content:
Hello All,

I'm trying to calculate the product cost rate in SAC but haven’t been able to get any results. I have four products — Product A, Product B, Product C, and Product D.

  • Product A and Product B are tagged to local sales GL accounts,

  • While Product B and Product C are tagged to export sales GL accounts,

  • This classification is based on the Distribution Channel property, where:

    • 10 = Local

    • 20 = Export

Each product has its own COGS values stored in the relevant leaf members of the following GL accounts:

  • 50301000 – COGS Direct Material

  • 50302000 – COGS Third Party

  • 50304000 – COGS Personnel Time

  • 50305000 – COGS Machine Time

  • 50307000 – COGS Production Overhead

Below is the code I'm executing to calculate product cost rate:

IF [d/SAP__FI_IFP_GLACCOUNT] = BASEMEMBER([d/SAP__FI_IFP_GLACCOUNT], "22") THEN // Gross Revenue
  DATA([d/Measures] = "QUANTITY", [d/Date] = #DATE) =
  RESULTLOOKUP([d/Measures] = "QUANTITY", [d/Version] = "public.Actual", [d/Date] = PREVIOUS(12)) *-1
ENDIF
 
IF [d/SAP__FI_IFP_GLACCOUNT] = BASEMEMBER([d/SAP__FI_IFP_GLACCOUNT], "21") THEN // COGS
DATA([d/Measures] = #AMOUNT, [d/Date] = #DATE) =
RESULTLOOKUP([d/Measures] = "AMOUNT", [d/Version] = "public.Actual",  [d/Date] = PREVIOUS(12))
ENDIF
 
IF [d/SAP__FI_IFP_GLACCOUNT] = BASEMEMBER([d/SAP__FI_IFP_GLACCOUNT], "21") THEN // COGS
DATA([d/Measures] = "PRICE") =
RESULTLOOKUP([d/Measures] = #AMOUNT, [d/Date] = #DATE) 
/ RESULTLOOKUP([d/Measures] = "QUANTITY", [d/Date] = #DATE)
 
ENDIF

 

Despite this logic, no results are returned, and I’m unsure why. Could someone please help identify the issue or suggest the correct approach?

 

Thank you in advance!

entry.title: Custom Widgets in SAP Analytics Cloud - Increased flexibility for Reporting and Planning
entry.content:
What is a SAC Custom Widget? 

The SAP Analytics Cloud offers companies a powerful platform for business intelligence, planning and predictive analyses. SAC provides a large number of standard widgets - so-called basic widgets - with which reports and dashboards can be designed quickly and effectively. Even though these basic widgets cover many common requirements and support a total of 21 different chart types, users sometimes come up against limitations when it comes to more specific visualization requirements. 

This is exactly where custom widgets come into play: these are individually developed visualization elements that go beyond what SAP offers as standard. A SAC custom widget is essentially a web component that consists of a JSON file for the description, JavaScript code for the behavior and optional additional resources such as CSS or images. This allows new chart types as well as customized user interface elements, interactions or even completely new functionalities to be implemented. 

 

GRAPHIC 1: EXAMPLE OF A CUSTOM WIDGET

 

 

Custom widgets offer developers full control: they can design their own diagrams and visualizations from scratch that are perfectly tailored to the specific requirements of a company - be it an interactive network diagram, a dynamic map view or a customized user interface. Thanks to the integration in SAC Stories and the support of the script API, custom widgets fit seamlessly into the existing environment and can be conveniently integrated into Stories via drag & drop. 

Custom widgets therefore allow the SAP Analytics Cloud to be expanded considerably - both in terms of visual design and functional depth. They are the ideal tool if the standard options are not sufficient and the focus is on individual visualization requirements. 

Application scenarios for custom widgets in the SAP Analytics Cloud 

Custom widgets open up a wide range of possible applications in the SAP Analytics Cloud, especially when standard widgets are not sufficient to meet specific requirements. A key area of application is the development of tailor-made visualizations that go beyond the 21 chart types available in the standard system. 

 

GRAPHIC 2: CUSTOM WIDGETS ARE AS INDIVIDUAL AS YOUR COMPANY'S REQUIREMENTS

 

For example, companies can create their own KPI tiles that contain numerical values as well as contextual information such as deviations or trend indicators. In addition, custom widgets enable the integration of interactive user interface elements such as calendars, date pickers or barcode generators that are required in specific business processes. The integration of SAPUI5 controls is also possible, allowing existing components to be reused and complex applications to be implemented efficiently. 

Another scenario is the extension of the SAC functionality by integrating external data sources or APIs. For example, custom widgets can visualize data from third-party systems in real time or enable specific interactions that cannot be realized with standard widgets. This offers companies the flexibility to tailor their dashboards and analyses precisely to their individual needs.  

Overall, custom widgets in the SAP Analytics Cloud offer a powerful way to extend standard functionalities and efficiently implement individual requirements. They are particularly advantageous when specific visualizations, user interactions or data integrations are required that go beyond the standard. 

Custom widgets in detail - how they work in the SAP Analytics Cloud 

Custom widgets can be seamlessly integrated into SAP Analytics Cloud Stories and behave like any other standard widget. After successful deployment, they are automatically listed in the widget menu so that they can be dragged and dropped onto the canvas and placed there. Once inserted, custom widgets can be flexibly moved, resized and visually designed - just like native components in SAC. 

 

GRAPHIC 3: ADD CUSTOM WIDGETS

 

Developers and users benefit from a wide range of functions. Basic properties such as color, font or frame can be adjusted via the styling panel. What is particularly interesting is that in the “Additional properties” section, you can add your own options that have been specially defined for the respective custom widget. Additional settings and functions can be stored in the builder panel, for example for data binding or initializing interactions. 

Custom widgets also support direct data binding, which means that they can be connected to data sources - a key requirement for interactive and data-driven visualizations. In the widget structure within a SAC story, custom widgets appear like any other component and can therefore be easily organized and targeted. 

Another powerful feature is the scripting capability: custom widgets can be addressed via the familiar SAC script API. In addition to the methods available as standard, you can also define and implement your own events - such as an onClick that triggers certain actions when the widget is clicked. This flexibility makes Custom Widgets a versatile tool that can be deeply integrated into SAC, both visually and functionally. 

Technical requirements and authorizations 

The development and use of custom widgets in the SAP Analytics Cloud requires specific technical prerequisites and authorizations. By default, SAP roles are restricted to read access. To create or update custom widgets, users must be assigned additional authorizations. These authorizations can either be set globally for all custom widgets or individually per widget. In particular, widget designers require the rights to create and update widgets. 

Several files are required to create a custom widget, including a JSON file to define the metadata and a JavaScript file for the functionality. These files are packed into a ZIP file together with optional resources such as CSS files or images. This ZIP file is then uploaded directly to the SAC, eliminating the need for external hosting. 

The development environment for custom widgets includes tools such as Visual Studio Code, a free editor from Microsoft that is particularly suitable for editing HTML and JavaScript. To create complex charts, developers can integrate free chart libraries such as ECharts, D3.js or Chart.js to realize advanced visualization options. 

 

GRAPHIC 4: DEVELOPMENT ENVIRONMENT

 

However, there are currently certain limitations when using custom widgets. For example, only Chromium-based browsers such as Google Chrome and Microsoft Edge are fully supported. In addition, the number of custom widgets that can be loaded into a single SAC tenant is limited to 50. 

Some features of the SAC are not supported by custom widgets. These include themes and CSS adjustments, pause refresh, commenting, scheduling, translations, data change insights and the “Search to Insight” function. Restrictions may also occur when exporting custom widgets to PDF, especially if certain chart libraries such as D3.js are used or if CSS settings are defined in the widget. 

There are numerous examples for references in the SAP Community and in SAP Help: 

  • SAP Analytics Cloud Custom Widget Developer Guide 
  • SAP Custom Widget examples on GitHub 
  • SAP Technology Blog: Custom Widgets & Widget Add-Ons 
  • Echarts library examples 
  • SAP Analytics Cloud Widget Add-On Developer Guide 
     

Your path to SAP Analytics Cloud Custom Widgets 

The introduction of custom widgets in the SAP Analytics Cloud offers companies an outstanding opportunity to expand their analytics solutions individually and to tailor them visually and functionally to specific requirements. To maximize the benefits, we recommend a structured and proven approach. 

  1. requirements analysis: In initial workshops, we work with you to analyze the technical, functional and content requirements for the planned custom widget. In this way, we ensure that all stakeholder needs are understood and taken into account - from IT to the specialist department. 
  2. proof of concept: We then develop an initial minimum viable product (MVP) to demonstrate the technical feasibility. This PoC serves as the basis for further decisions and for internal coordination with relevant teams. 
  3. implementation: Based on the PoC, we add the required functionalities to the widget, test it in your SAC environment and hand over the finished custom widget as part of a clean documentation and handover. 
  4.  

GRAPHIC 5: EVALUATION

 

Custom widgets are a modular building block for making a variety of advanced functions usable in SAC - from individual visualizations to complex interactions. They promote acceptance among end users and enable customized solutions for company-specific challenges. At the same time, you should consider the increased development effort compared to standard widgets and the possible training requirements for your team - because individual developments also require internal know-how in the long term.

entry.title: How to determine business content would be or has been updated
entry.content:
Hello all,

Does Business Content updates, ACTUALLY update an original (i.e. overwrite), or are updates always new versions (i.e. SAP_xxxxxx_V2).

Let me take this example. "Finance Foundation for SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud", what changes are made in version 1.6.1?

Ideally, I wish to pick and choose the artefacts that have been udpated? How can I see what updates are in this version?
